title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-20, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fkrebsonsecurity.com%2F2026%2F05%2Fcisa-admin-leaked-aws-govcloud-keys-on-github%2F%3Futm_source=tldrinfosec/1/0100019e457f6100-f041e421-7c79-4ab0-908b-c541cb593e60-000000/EL_Gk5n-nAiCAl4FpnCgxWNhMe4fc7cW_kbOt6agIrM=452
summary: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github (4 minute read) A CISA contractor used a public GitHub repo as a working scratchpad, exposing AWS GovCloud admin keys, plaintext passwords, and internal tooling details for months. Weak passwords and disabled GitHub secret-scanning point to basic hygiene and oversight failures at an already understaffed agency.

title: The Evolution of Cassandra Data Movement at Netflix
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FYxD1OG/1/0100019e4a0319c8-748980c9-c1c6-4c23-8de8-bce0341f5604-000000/0J8CznD-an-dwRQbCJ1PQLO1g1IfcMCs_0FfTakGr7M=452
summary: The Evolution of Cassandra Data Movement at Netflix (8 minute read) Netflix replaced its Cassandra-to-Iceberg movement engine with a layered platform that reads backups directly from S3, converts them to Spark DataFrames, and lets each data abstraction build its own optimized connector. title: mondayDB 3 – Solving HTAP for a Trillion-Table System
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 21 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fengineering.monday.com%2Fmondaydb-3-solving-htap-for-a-trillion-table-system%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e4a0319c8-748980c9-c1c6-4c23-8de8-bce0341f5604-000000/UZGNm-rEYKUmdp1YK__DRH-lteCr4DHhSpy3ywdHi4I=452
summary: mondayDB 3 – Solving HTAP for a Trillion-Table System (21 minute read) mondayDB 3 is an HTAP system designed to handle over a trillion dynamic, constantly evolving tables with highly flexible schemas. They replaced their MySQL + JSON architecture with a CQRS-based Lambda architecture powered by DuckDB: immutable snapshots in object storage, an external WAL for real-time changes, and a soft-stateful serving layer that syncs + queries local DuckDB files on every read.

summary: Returning to life! (6 minute read) AI is both genuinely empowering for data science. It makes programming, translation, voice input, and broad learning more accessible. However, it is genuinely harmful through environmental cost, copyright issues, wealth concentration, shallow thinking, and unequal access. The tension cannot be neatly resolved, but data science leaders still need to engage with AI seriously so they can help people use it well.

title: Better Experiments with LLM Evals — A funnel, not a fork
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fengineering.atspotify.com%2F2026%2F5%2Fbetter-experiments-with-llm-evals-a-funnel-not-a-fork%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e4a0319c8-748980c9-c1c6-4c23-8de8-bce0341f5604-000000/XQdkGJjp2uJl5cWiIgR0hmUvTY9uhDsLnWcwLzbN_Yk=452
summary: Better Experiments with LLM Evals — A funnel, not a fork (4 minute read) Treat LLM evals and online A/B experiments as a funnel by using LLM judges early to verify quality (relevance, tone, and coherence) and filter out weak ideas before they consume experiment resources. This raises the success rate of experiments. Running evals on experiment results creates a feedback loop that continuously calibrates and improves the judges themselves.

title: What's Easy Now? What's Hard Now?
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fbrooker.co.za%2Fblog%2F2026%2F05%2F18%2Fwhats-easy-whats-hard.html%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e4a0319c8-748980c9-c1c6-4c23-8de8-bce0341f5604-000000/3x1fMGbAGYmM6Yyx5x0MgcMs8n4QwVQENlMj53tyCqk=452
summary: What's Easy Now? What's Hard Now? (4 minute read) The long-term capabilities of coding agents will be determined more by the quality of feedback loops than by raw model intelligence. Tasks with fast, accurate, automated feedback (e.g. building high-performance databases with formal specs) will become surprisingly “easy” for agents, while tasks reliant on slow, subjective human feedback will remain relatively “hard.”

title: Monitoring Cortex Agent Performance With Trace Data
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fmontecarlo.ai%2Fblog-monitoring-cortex-agent-performance-with-trace-data%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e4a0319c8-748980c9-c1c6-4c23-8de8-bce0341f5604-000000/BOrbImfSSHSb447HHEZOwfMrhcXad8FI3Eqf3Jp-4W8=452
summary: Monitoring Cortex Agent Performance With Trace Data (8 minute read) By querying structured observability events and tracking key metrics at the span level, such as token consumption, duration/latency, and error rates (planning, tool calls, and response generation), teams can effectively monitor agents in production to detect issues like context bloat in multi-turn conversations, token spikes, slow tool calls, or shifts in question complexity.

title: Protocols for transactional usage of object storage
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.bitsxpages.com%2Fp%2Fprotocols-for-transactional-usage%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e4a0319c8-748980c9-c1c6-4c23-8de8-bce0341f5604-000000/abg7gD6kbXXi6-t5j1s7PLa7ukQwLN-q4qVHkJaJf40=452
summary: Protocols for transactional usage of object storage (8 minute read) Object storage can support serializable OLTP if you build on three write primitives (atomic PUTs, conditional PUT If-Match/If-None-Match, and strongly consistent LISTs) and three read primitives (atomic GETs, conditional GET If-None-Match, and consistent listing). The core tradeoff is safety versus contention cost, and you need safe garbage collection to prevent the storage from growing indefinitely.

title: How to Write "Garbage Code"
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 7 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fread.engineerscodex.com%2Fp%2Fhow-to-not-write-garbage-code-by%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e4a4a0475-3accf979-d86a-48bd-8836-710c2952d4e0-000000/giIOfG94u5PVEiG63j0-1Cy-3rXLtY_tDE0NRjphn94=452
summary: How to Write "Garbage Code" (by Linus Torvalds) (7 minute read) Linus Torvalds, the creator of Git and Linux, says that overly complex or abstracted code, like unnecessary helper functions, makes code maintainability and understanding worse. It increases cognitive burden and, in some cases, it makes sense to repeat code to reduce human and LLM context switching. Code should be optimized for readability and ease of iteration.

comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-21, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fcekrem.github.io%2Fposts%2Fthe-tacit-dimension%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e4a4a0475-3accf979-d86a-48bd-8836-710c2952d4e0-000000/jd4vELe0ma4Bo45gQzzFGj3eosUEzQe1AdW6xJvWUtc=452
summary: The Tacit Dimension: Why Your Best Engineers Can't Tell You What They Know (12 minute read) Software engineering expertise relies on tacit knowledge, an intuitive understanding that AI cannot grasp since it is only trained on explicit text and code. title: Twelve Ways to Be Wrong About AI-Assisted Coding
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-22, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fthird-bit.com%2F2026%2F05%2F20%2Ftwelve-ways-to-be-wrong%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdevops/1/0100019e4f72e248-7e35ea0f-3a2e-4810-9dfd-ae45acdf7e1f-000000/3IwvVscroFveTTVyDSuvA0f0zHPmxDKNbussE26xpH8=452
summary: Twelve Ways to Be Wrong About AI-Assisted Coding (8 minute read) Measuring AI coding productivity is easy to get wrong: lines of code, adoption rates, ticket counts, self-reported productivity, and toy-task speedups often measure activity or novelty rather than real long-term value. Better evaluation needs control groups, system-level metrics, long-term observation, and accounting for hidden costs like review burden, security issues, technical debt, and increased maintenance.

Success depends on earning trust by knowing when to help, when to stay silent, and protecting privacy.
